coredata.dc:title: Exploring the incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) on polyethersulfone (PES) membrane with dual-responsive self-healing property for water filtration

coredata.dc:description: In recent years, self-healing materials have been widely discussed in various applications. A water-responsive polyethersulfone (PES)/polyethyleneimine (PEI)/poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) membrane was successfully fabricated for water filtration and exhibited its self-healing ability, self-healing efficiency and filtration performance is not satisfactory and requires further investigation became the interest. This study aims to further improve the performance of the water-responsive self-healing membranes by incorporating amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and introducing a dual-responsive (water and heat) self-healing mechanism. The surface functional groups and morphology analysis of NCQDs were examined, and the surface analysis of the fabricated membranes was determined. In this study, the self-healing efficiency and tartrazine recovery of fabricated NCQDs membranes have improved to 91.1 % and 63.3 %, respectively when the damaged membrane was healed in a dual-responsive mechanism for 6 h. Nonetheless, the pure water flux (PWF) rose by 47.6 % to 33.8 L/m2.h and the tartrazine rejection improved from 71.9 % to 87.9 % when 0.6 vol% and 0.4 vol% of NCQDs were added separately into the PEI and PAA solutions. These findings suggest that adding NCQDs and involving a dual-responsive self-healing mechanism could improve the membrane healing capability and enhance its overall performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, self-healing materials have been widely discussed in various applications. A water-responsive polyethersulfone (PES)/polyethyleneimine (PEI)/poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) membrane was successfully fabricated for water filtration and exhibited its self-healing ability, self-healing efficiency and filtration performance is not satisfactory and requires further investigation became the interest. This study aims to further improve the performance of the water-responsive self-healing membranes by incorporating amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and introducing a dual-responsive (water and heat) self-healing mechanism. The surface functional groups and morphology analysis of NCQDs were examined, and the surface analysis of the fabricated membranes was determined. In this study, the self-healing efficiency and tartrazine recovery of fabricated NCQDs membranes have improved to 91.1 % and 63.3 %, respectively when the damaged membrane was healed in a dual-responsive mechanism for 6 h. Nonetheless, the pure water flux (PWF) rose by 47.6 % to 33.8 L/m2.h and the tartrazine rejection improved from 71.9 % to 87.9 % when 0.6 vol% and 0.4 vol% of NCQDs were added separately into the PEI and PAA solutions. These findings suggest that adding NCQDs and involving a dual-responsive self-healing mechanism could improve the membrane healing capability and enhance its overall performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the applications of self-healing materials have intensively risen in various industries such as biomedical, food packaging, multilevel thin film, electronic sensors and batteries [1–4]. Many studies have been conducted to understand the fundamental healing mechanism of a self-healing material which has been critically reviewed by Wong et al. (2023) [5]. In general, the healing effect of a self-healing material was based on either extrinsic or intrinsic mechanisms. The latter intrinsic mode is preferred due to its repeating-healing ability compared to the single-use extrinsic mode [6–8]. With the successful development of self-healable materials, the concept of self-healing was further extended into membrane technology.

A “smart membrane”, known as a self-healing membrane is made to be self-healed after damage so that it can be reused over an extended period. Research on the single stimulus-responsive self-healing membrane has been widely conducted and discussed, for example, thermal-responsive [9–11], water-responsive [12–14], pH-responsive [15–18] and light-responsive [19]. Apart from the single-responsive self-healing mechanism, some studies have reported on the dual-responsive self-healing mechanism with enhanced performance [20–23]. For instance, Banerjee and Singha (2017) successfully fabricated a self-healing hydrogel with a dual-responsive self-healing mechanism. The hydrogel was able to heal in both thermal and water stimuli. The damaged hydrogel swelled faster when in contact with water at higher temperatures, causing molecular diffusion and chain mobility that subsequently healed the damaged region [23]. Noting the potential of the dual-responsive mechanism, the involvement of multi-stimuli simultaneously in the membrane filtration process is still uncertain.

Membrane technology in water filtration is well known for its low energy consumption and cost-effectiveness which has arisen as the simple and effective method for dye removal to increase the availability of clean water [24,25]. The disadvantages of this technology such as brittleness and fragility of the membrane greatly impacted the membrane performance. The self-healing membrane has emerged as the solution to resolve the challenges such as physical damage and membrane fouling encountered in membrane technology [26].

Previously, a water-responsive self-healed membrane was successfully fabricated through surface modification of polyethersulfone (PES) with polyelectrolytes for the water filtration process [25]. In the study, the membrane was cut physically using a microtome blade. The damaged membrane was healed for 6 h in contact with water to form hydrogen bonding for the self-healing process. The healing effectiveness of 75.9 % from pure water flux (PWF) was achieved. Meanwhile, the tartrazine rejection rose by 95.6 % from the original PES membrane. Study has successfully demonstrated the potential of a self-healing membrane in membrane filtration. However, the self-healing effectiveness of the membrane has always been a concern for the ability to recover fully or partially the membrane's original properties in terms of water permeation and solute rejection [25].

Studies have been reported on the various surface modifications such as incorporating nanomaterials to enhance the membrane performances regarding hydrophilicity, self-healing effectiveness and solute rejection [27–29]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a newly emerging carbon nanomaterial with spherical and amorphous nanocrystalline nuclei of diameter between 2 and 20 nm. The dimensionless CDs contain many surface functional groups. The epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups help in the distinct properties of CDs such as excellent aqueous solubility, chemical stability, and biocompatibility [30,31]. Since the first discovery of CDs, various studies have been conducted on the synthesizing method, its properties and their applications. The successful incorporation of CDs as carbon nanomaterials has further diverged into other applications such as water and wastewater treatment, sensors, electrochemistry and the medical field which has been reviewed in-depth by Yan et al. (2021) [32].

In the study of Gan et al. (2020), the PES membrane for tartrazine removal was prepared by adding silver/CQDs. The permeability improved by about 63 % and tartrazine rejection increased by 31 % at 0.5 wt% of silver/CQDs. From the study, the water flux increased when the nanoparticles were at the optimum dosage and decreased when the dosage was beyond the optimum due to the agglomeration of nanoparticles [28]. The rejection and anti-fouling properties were also enhanced with the nanoparticle loading. Moreover, Heng et al. (2021) has developed a visible-light responsive photocatalytic membrane modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NQCDs) and titanium(IV) oxide (TiO2) for the filtration and dye degradation in wastewater treatment. From the study, the rejection performance increased and the water flux decreased when higher dosages of nanoparticles were used due to the clogging of pores from the aggregation of nanoparticles [19]. According to Heng et al. (2021), the presence of nitrogen as the doping element enhanced the quantum yield and photoluminescence of the CQDs [30]. Additionally, the amine groups in graphene oxide (GO) nanoparticles have shown their self-healing ability [33]. Several studies have demonstrated that the amine groups helped in self-healing performance through the formation of hydrogen bonding [34,35]. Although the incorporation of nanoparticles certainly improved membrane performance and properties, the optimum dosages of nanoparticles may vary according to the materials and conditions of the polymer matrix.

To the best of our knowledge, no study has been conducted on NCQDs nanoparticles incorporated into polyelectrolyte multilayer self-healing PES membranes with a dual-responsive self-healing mechanism. We herein report the incorporation of NCQDs into the polyelectrolyte layers by identifying the optimum NCQDs loading in the PEI and PAA solutions to observe the changes in membrane performance. The effect of the dual-responsive (water and heat) self-healing mechanism on the fabricated membrane is also evaluated on the self-healing effectiveness. Then, the self-healing capability on different damage loads is examined to study the strength of the NQCDs-polyelectrolyte layers to self-heal. The membrane performance was analyzed for their PWF, tartrazine rejection, tartrazine recovery and self-healing effectiveness. Additionally, the salt rejection was tested for monovalent and divalent anions while the pH stability of the membrane was studied in acidic and alkaline conditions. Nonetheless, the anti-fouling performance of the NCQDs incorporated membrane was explored.

To prepare NCQDs nanoparticles, isopropanol (IPA, C3H8O, Mw = 60.1 g/mol, 99 %) and ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H8N2, Mw = 60.1 g/mol, 99 %) were procured from Merck while the palm oil frond (POF) was collected from an oil palm estate in Tanjung Tualang, Perak. The materials for fabricating the self-healing membrane were similar to our previous study [25]. Furthermore, tartrazine (dye content ≥ 85 %, Mw = 534.3 g/mol) from Sigma-Aldrich was used as the synthetic dye. While, sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, Mw = 142.04 g/mol), magnesium sulphate (MgSO4, Mw = 120.37 g/mol) and sodium chloride (NaCl, Mw = 58.44 g/mol) were purchased from Merck for salt rejection. Furthermore, nitric acid (HNO3, Mw = 63.01 g/mol, 65 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Mw = 39.997 g/mol) solutions from Merck and R&M Chemicals were used for the pH stability test of the fabricated membrane and sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl, Mw: 74.44 g/mol, 10 %, Sigma Aldrich) was used as the cleaning agent. All chemicals were used as received.

The NCQDs nanoparticle was synthesized according to the protocol as reported in the study of Heng et al. (2020) [36]. Firstly, 2.5 g of nano-sized powdered POFs was mixed with 75 mL isopropanol (IPA) and 1 mL ethylenediamine (EDA), together with 25 mL distilled water. The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min to obtain a homogenized solution. The solution (pH ~ 12) was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The heated solution was then cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter and vacuum filtration to remove large particles. The obtained brown dispersion was dialysed using a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 2.0 kDa) against distilled water for three days for NCQDs purification. The purified NCQDs aqueous dispersion was freeze-dried at −110 °C and the NCQDs powder was collected for further characterization and use. The obtained nanoparticle powder was re-dispersed into distilled water with a concentration of about 40 mg/mL and was kept in the dark at 4 °C before use.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was conducted by the drop-casting method using 96 % ethanol to provide detailed images of NCDQ nanoparticles by detecting the transmission of electrons through the sample. The absorption spectrum of NCQDs was analyzed using UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) for the range of 200–800 nm wavenumber. While the functional groups of the NCQDs were detected using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning from 400 to 4000 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PEI/PAA membrane with NCQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The pre-treatment of the PES membrane support was similar to our previous study [25]. Different NCQD loadings (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 by volume percent, vol%) were added into the PEI solution to explore the changes in the membrane performance. Firstly, 0.2 vol% of the filtered NCQD was pipetted into 4 mL of the prepared 5 mg/mL PEI solution. The mixture was homogenously stirred overnight. Then, the NCQDs-PEI mixture was transferred onto the cleaned PES membrane support for the deposition process. After 15 min, the membrane was washed lightly with distilled water and immediately deposited about 4 mL of pure PAA solution, followed by rinsing with distilled water. The membrane was placed in the oven at 60 °C for half an hour. The deposition procedure is presented in the schematic diagram as shown in Fig. A1 [25]. The prepared membrane labelled as PES/0.2NCQDs-PEI/PAA was ready for the next filtration study. The experiment was replicated and the membranes were denoted as PES/xNCQDs-PEI/PAA where x represented the NCQD loadings into the PEI solution.

After identifying the optimum NCQD loadings on the PEI solution, the effect of different NCQD loadings (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 by volume percent, vol%) on the PAA solution was studied. The membrane fabrication method was similar to Section 2.4.1. The optimum xNCQDs-PEI solution from the previous section was first deposited on the membrane. Then, 0.2 vol% of NCQDs was pipetted into the 5 mg/mL PAA solution, followed by the deposition and rinsing procedure. The prepared PES/xNCQDs-PEI/0.2NCQDs-PAA membranes were analyzed for their PWF, self-healing efficiency and solute rejection. The experiment was repeated and the symbol “y” represented the NCQDs loading into the PAA solution and labelled as PES/xNCQDs-PEI/yNCQDs-PAA.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) were used to visualize the membrane surface. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was employed to identify the surface functional groups of the fabricated membranes, at the range of 400 to 4000 cm−1. The water contact angle (WCA) was measured using the sessile drop method. Furthermore, the membrane porosity, ε was computed through the gravimetric method. The wet membrane was weighed as
W 1

where n is the water viscocity, Q is the volume of permeate, and Δ P is the transmembrane pressure.

The prepared membrane was first compacted at 4 bar in a lab-scale dead-end cell for 20 min, followed by filtration with distilled water and namely PWF before-damaged. Then, a microtome blade with a constant 100 g load was used to cut the membrane and immediately subjected to a filtration process, denoted as PWF after-damaged. Next, the damaged membrane was healed in distilled water (water-responsive) for 6 h. After the healing process, the membrane was placed into the dead-end cell for another filtration process and indicated as PWF after-healed [25]. The experiment was then repeated for different damaged loads (50, 150, 300, 500 g). Later, a dual-responsive self-healing mechanism was applied to heal the damaged membrane in distilled water at 60 °C for 6 and 24 h to study the effect of single and dual-responsive healing mechanisms on the membrane performance. Finally, the PWF and healing efficiency of the membrane was computed using Eqs. (3) and (4) respectively:

f native is the PWF difference between pre and after-damaged.

For the rejection test, 50 mg/L of tartrazine dye was transferred into the dead-end cell for the filtration test. The procedure was followed as in Section 2.6.1. The concentration of the tartrazine dye in the feed and permeate was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 427 nm [25]. Additionally, salt rejection of the fabricated membrane was conducted using 5 mM of NaCl, Na2SO4 and MgSO4. By using a conductivity meter, the salt's conductivity, (mS/m) was measured. The tartrazine rejection and salt rejection, R (%) were then calculated using Eq. (5) [14]:

Furthermore, the stability membrane was tested using the solutions of different pH conditions. The membrane was first tested for the initial PWF for comparison. The membrane was then immersed in 0.1 M HNO3 (pH 4) for a day, followed by rinsing with distilled water and carried out the PWF test. The pH stability was analyzed using the initial PWF and PWF after 2 weeks of immersion in the solution. The experiment was repeated for a neutral solution (pH 7) distilled water (pH 7) and an alkaline solution of 0.1 M NaOH (pH 10).

To study the membrane's anti-fouling property, 50 mg/L of tartrazine dye, similar to the rejection test was used as the influent. Firstly, the membrane was subjected to PWF test and denoted as

J dye

Fig. 1 illustrates the absorption spectrum of NCQDs using UV–vis spectroscopy and two inherent peaks were observed at 275 nm and 354 nm. According to Atchudan et al. (2019), the peak at 275 nm corresponded to the π-π* transition sp2-hybridized carbon‑carbon double bond structure while the 354 nm peak was correlated to the n-π* transition of carbonyl (C=O) or imine (C=N) in the NCQDs structure [37]. The inset shows the images of yellowish-brown NCQDs under ambient light and bright green fluorescence NCQDs under UV light, proving the successful synthesis of NCQDs from POF.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM surface analysis, selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern and lattice spacing of the NCQDs prepared. From Fig. 2a, the NCQDs exhibit uniform and quasi-spherical nanoparticles with dimensions between 2.8 and 4.5 nm. These dimensions are in the range of CQDs, suggesting the successful formation of NCQDs [19,37]. Furthermore, Fig. 2b shows the crystalline structure of the NCQDs nanoparticles with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm [38].

The functional groups of the NCQDs, pristine PES, fabricated PES/PEI/PAA and NCQDs membranes are depicted in Fig. 3 . The characteristic peaks of NCQDs are observed at 2929 cm−1, 1650 cm−1 and 1438 cm−1 which attributes to the CH, CO and CN groups respectively in the structure [37]. Additionally, a broad band of 3100–3600 cm−1 is observed at NCQDs spectrum, indicating the presence of hydroxyl (OH) groups. From Fig. 3, the presence of NCQD characteristic peaks that can be observed from the intense peak in the PES/0.6NCQDs-PEI/0.4NCQDs-PAA spectrum suggests the membrane was successfully modified with NCQDs. These peaks are not obvious in the PES/0.6NCQDs-PEI/PAA spectrum implying that the NCQDs in the inner PEI layer cannot be fully detected, but only surface functional groups of the membrane are identified.

EDX elemental analysis of the membranes was examined and the results are tabulated in Table 1 . The basic elements of the pristine PES membrane include carbon (71.56 %), oxygen (16.89 %), sulphur (9.81 %) and nitrogen (1.74 %). The atomic percentage of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements increased for all the fabricated membranes, which corresponds to the carboxylic (-COOH) groups of PAA and amine (−NH2) groups of PEI [25]. Apart from the functional groups of the polyelectrolytes, the functional groups of NCQDs, as confirmed in the FTIR analysis, also contribute to the increment in the atomic percentage [28,39]. Meanwhile, the decrement in the atomic percentage of element sulphur for all the fabricated membranes indicates the successful deposition of polyelectrolytes on the PES membrane.

The WCA was examined to study the hydrophilicity of the NCQDs membrane and is presented in Table 2 . The WCA of the PEI/PAA membrane without NCQDs exhibited the highest of 60.2 o, indicating the surface of the hydrophobic PES membrane (WCA = 90.2 o) has been modified to a hydrophilic surface [25]. The incorporation of NCQDs into the membrane matrix further decreased the WCA as shown in Table 2, suggesting an improvement in the surface hydrophilicity due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups from NCQDs. According to the study of Mulyati et al. (2020), the membrane hydrophilicity enhanced the PWF by decreasing the hydraulic resistance [40]. Furthermore, the membrane pore size is also evaluated. Table 2 shows that the pore size of the pristine PES membrane (98.03 nm) decreased when the polyelectrolytes and NCQDs-polyelectrolytes were deposited on the membrane. From Table 2, the pore size increased when the NCQD loadings in the PEI solution were increased. Then, the pore size started to reduce when the NCQD loadings in the PEI solution exceeded 0.6 vol%. The pore size further decreased when NCQD was added to the PAA solution on top of the NQCDs-PEI layer. The trend of the pore size was similar to the PWF analysis. According to He et al. (2024), the hydrophilic CQDs are favourable for water permeation. The increasing NCQDs loadings contribute more hydrophilic functional groups on the membrane surface, enhancing internal polarity and creating interfacial voids for the increasing PWF [41]. As discussed earlier in the WCA analysis, the hydrophilicity of the membranes increased with increasing NCQDs loadings. This observation was similar to the study of Feng et al. (2021), which showed that hydrophilicity is the contributing factor in improving PWF [42]. However, several studies reported that the appropriate amount of nanoparticles increases membrane pore size to achieve better water permeation until the nanoparticles reach a certain limit. The nanoparticles accumulated and covered the membrane pore, subsequently decreasing the water permeation [43–45]. These findings revealed the role of nanoparticles in designing the membrane with desirable pore size [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different NCQDs loading on PEI
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 4a, the PWF of the prepared NCQD membranes shows a bell-shaped curve where the highest PWF was marked at 0.6 vol% NCQD loadings. From the figure, there was an increment of about 67.2 % PWF from 17.7 L/m2.h to 54.0 L/m2.h when the NCQDs increased from zero loading to 0.6 vol% on the PEI solution. According to Yan et al. (2021), incorporating CDs into a membrane significantly enhanced the permeation flux due to the tuneable hydrophilicity of CDs [32]. The surface functional groups of CDs such as hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups contribute to the increasing PWF by forming hydrogen bonds between water molecules. The physical hydrogen bonding strengthened the driving force for water molecules to channel through the membrane [28]. In this study, the CDs were functionalized with the hydrophilic amine groups, referred to as the NCQDs, which improved the hydrophilicity and water adsorption of the membrane through hydrogen bonds, which was in line with the WCA and PWF analysis. Moreover, the presence of carboxyl groups in the PAA also contributed to the water permeation due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between carboxyl groups of PAA and water molecules. However, the PWF decreased from 32.2 L/m2.h to 19.1 L/m2.h when the NCQDs loading increased from 0.8 vol% to 1.0 vol%. This observation could be due to agglomeration of NCQDs nanoparticles. In the study of Koulivand et al. (2020), nanoparticles agglomerated at higher dosages and subsequently decreased membrane pore size. SEM analysis was performed for surface images of the fabricated membranes (Fig. A2). Despite the smooth surface at different NCQDs loading in the PEI solution, no obvious difference was observed as the last deposited layer was PAA for all the membranes. As for the pure PEI/PAA membrane (no NCQDs loading), only a slight change in PWF was observed between 0.2 vol% NCQDs, from 17.7 L/m2.h to 18.3 L/m2.h as the nanoparticles loading could be too little to reveal the role of NCQDs in enhancing water flux.

Apart from the PWF analysis, the addition of nanoparticles certainly improves the self-healing effectiveness of the membrane as shown in Fig. 4a. The self-healing efficiency improved from 69.2 % (0.2 vol% NCQDs) to 85.0 % (1.0 vol% NCQDs). As reported in several studies, hydrogen bonding is responsible for the intrinsic self-healing process in the presence of water molecules [46,47]. When the membrane was damaged, voids occurred at the damaged site. The water molecules fused into the voids when the membrane was immersed in distilled water. The water molecules then interacted with all the hydrophilic functional groups, forming hydrogen bonds, followed by the swelling of the polyelectrolyte layers, which subsequently healed the damaged site. In this study, the functional groups of PEI and NCQDs surrounding the damaged site were exposed to distilled water when the membrane was cut and the amounts of functional groups exposed increased with increasing NCQDs loading. This resulted in more hydrogen bonds formed during the healing process and consequently improved the self-healing effectiveness of the fabricated membrane.

Additionally, the tartrazine rejection of the fabricated membranes at different NCQDs loading on the PEI solution was studied and the recovery of rejections after-damaged and after-healed was reported as illustrated in Fig. 4b. It was observed that the tartrazine rejection improved along with NCQDs loading. The highest rejection of 84.8 % was achieved at 1.0 vol% of NCQDs loading while a lower rejection of 72.9 % was observed at 0.2 vol% NCQDs loading. In contrast, there was about 71.9 % tartrazine rejection at no NCQDs loading membrane. The rejection performance was first explained by the last deposited PAA layer and anionic tartrazine through the electrostatic repulsion. The negatively charged carboxylic groups in the PAA repelled the anionic tartrazine from permeating through the membrane [28]. This observation was consistent with the study of Gan et al. (2020) where the negatively charged membrane removed the anionic tartrazine.

Nonetheless, the rejection performance of the healed membrane after immersing in water showed an increasing trend with NCQDs loading. The highest rejection of about 49.0 % was achieved at 1.0 vol% of NCQDs loading, followed by 43.1 % (0.8 vol% NCQDs) and 40.1 % (0.6 vol% NCQDs). While 31.7 % and 35.5 % of tartrazine rejection after healing were observed at 0.2 and 0.4 vol% NCQDs. And, the lowest rejection after healing (27.1 %) was marked at no NCQDs loading membrane. Furthermore, the recovery performance increased with NCQDs loadings. This could be due to the reformation of hydrogen bonds between the hydrophilic groups with water molecules when in contact with water, retaining the tartrazine on the membrane surface [48].

Based on the study, the PWF increased with the increment of NCQDs and reached the maximum at 0.6 vol% NCQDs loading. The membrane's self-healing efficiency, solute rejection and recovery showed a promising result at 0.6 vol% NCQDs loading. As the NCQDs agglomerate at higher concentration, the NCQDs loading of 0.6 vol% was chosen for further study after considering the overall membrane performances and was labelled as PES/0.6NCQDs-PEI/PAA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different NCQDs loading on PAA
ce:section.ce:para: The PES/0.6NCQDs-PEI/yNCQDs-PAA membrane was fabricated to study the effect of NCQDs in the PAA solution for the possible contribution to the membrane performance. The SEM surface analysis (Fig. A3) showed a smooth surface when the NCQDs were added to the PAA solution and deposited as the last layer on the membrane. As shown in Fig. 5a, the PWF dropped from the highest of 54.0 L/m2.h to the lowest of 8.7 L/m2.h when the NCQDs loading was increased from 0.0 to 1.0 vol% in the PAA solution. This observation explained the agglomeration of NCQDs nanoparticles at higher dosages as discussed in Section 3.3. On the other hand, the healing efficiencies of all NCQD loadings on PAA were relatively consistent at about 80 %. This finding suggested that the layer of NCQDs-PEI was in charge of the healing efficiency of the NCQDs polyelectrolyte membrane. This observation was in line with Section 3.3 where the healing efficiency of the NCQDs-PEI membranes reached the maximum of about 85 %. In contrast, the layer of NCQDs-PAA was in charge of the rejection performance. From Fig. 5b, the tartrazine rejection showed an increasing trend when the NCQDs loading in the PAA solution was increased. This was due to the dominant electrostatic repulsion force between the negatively charged NCQDs and functional groups of PAA. High amounts of negatively charged functional groups at the terminating layer blocked the tartrazine from passing through the membrane due to the electrostatic repulsion [28,49]. Additionally, Khdary et al. (2023) reported that higher surface charges at the membrane create electrostatic repulsion and prevent membrane fouling [50].

Furthermore, it was observed that the tartrazine recovery reached a plateau by a maximum of 50 % at 0.8 vol% NCQDs in the PAA solution after the damaged membrane was healed in distilled water. This result could be due to the maximum amount of polyelectrolytes that have been used up for the healing process. From the result above, the NCQDs loading in the PAA solution of 0.4 vol%, indicated as PES/0.6NCQDs-PEI/0.4NCQDs-PAA, was chosen for further study with the optimum performances based on PWF, self-healing effectiveness, tartrazine rejection and tartrazine recovery of 33.8 L/m2.h, 80.4 %, 85.5 % and 43.7 % respectively.

Previously, the membrane without NCQDs was healed in a single water-responsive mechanism at room temperature and the self-healing effectiveness achieved about 75.9 % as illustrated in Fig. 6a. After selecting the optimum NCQDs loading in the PEI (0.6 vol% NCQDs) and PAA (0.4 vol% NCQDs) solution, the membrane was tested again for its self-healing efficiency at different healing conditions. At 6 h of healing time, the damaged membrane was successfully healed to 80.4 % of its PWF. The self-healing efficiency was improved only slightly to 82.2 % when the membrane was immersed overnight in distilled water at room temperature. This finding was similar to the study of Wypych (2022) where the healing efficiency of the polymeric membrane improved with healing time [51]. The increment in healing efficiency can be further explained by the reforming of hydrogen bonds between the negatively charged functional groups of PAA and NCQDs with the water molecule [28,47]. In our previous study, the optimum drying temperature of 60 °C was sufficient to stabilize the polyelectrolyte layer without causing any cracks on the membrane surface [25]. Therefore, the temperature of 60 °C was used as the thermal stimulus, concurrently with distilled water as the water stimulus for the dual-responsive self-healing mechanism. The dual-responsive mechanism was studied on the self-healing efficiency of the fabricated NCQDs membrane. As shown in Fig. 6a, the self-healing effectiveness of a dual-responsive self-healing membrane improved significantly to over 90 %. Comparing the performance at 6 h of healing time for a single water-responsive and a dual-responsive mechanism, the former has a self-healing efficiency of 80.4 % than the latter of 91.1 %. A similar trend was observed at 24 h of healing time, where the self-healing efficiency rose from 82.2 % to 92.7 %.

In terms of the rejection recovery performance, the fabricated membrane without NCQDs has a recovery of 35.8 % and improved to 43.7 % after adding NCQDs at 6 h of immersion in distilled water at room conditions. In contrast, the recovery enhanced drastically to 63.3 % and 66.0 % at 6 h and 24 h of healing times when a dual-responsive self-healing mechanism was implied. The self-healing behaviour was governed by the formation of reversible hydrogen bonds as discussed earlier, together with the large number of polyelectrolyte bonds formed in the presence of temperature, which subsequently led to the thicker membrane network and further increased the self-healing efficiency as shown in Fig. 6a. According to Cai et al. (2023), the self-healing effectiveness was improved with temperature as it strengthens the polymer network for rapid healing during heating [20]. These findings successfully revealed the potential dual-responsive self-healing mechanism in the membrane technology. Despite that, all the PWF and rejection before damaged of the fabricated NCQDs membranes showed consistent results, indicating the consistency in the fabrication method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-healing capabilities at different damage loads
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the newly self-healing NCQD membrane was subjected to different damage loads to observe the self-healing capabilities in a dual-responsive healing effect. The physical image of the damaged membrane is attached in Fig. A4. From Fig. 7a, the healing efficiency dropped with increasing damage loads but managed to sustain above 70 % of healing efficiency. At 50 g loads, the healing efficiency has its highest of about 99.7 %, followed by 100 g (92.7 %), 150 g (88.9 %), 300 g (80.5 %) and the lowest of 70.8 % at 500 g (70.8 %). The surface images of the healed membranes are illustrated in Fig. A5. At lower damage loads, the damaged area was relatively small, the healing effect was less resistant, and the polyelectrolytes in the undamaged area migrated to repair the damaged area, subsequently leading to higher self-healing efficiency [13]. Fig. A6 visualised the SEM images of damaged and healed membranes at 100 g load. For instance, the PWF increased from 35.6 L/m2.h to 65.4 L/m2.h when a 50 g load was physically cut through the membrane. Then, the PWF recovered to 36.0 L/m2.h after healing in distilled water at 60 °C for a day.

In addition, the tartrazine recovery showed a decreasing trend from the highest of 72.0 % to the lowest of 47.7 % (500 g) as illustrated in Fig. 7b. The tartrazine rejection decreased from 85.9 % to 22.4 % when a load of 50 g was applied and increased to 80.0 % when the damaged membrane was healed. In contrast, the PWF increased drastically at 500 g load from 34.1 L/m2.h to 126.3 L/m2.h after damage, indicating the membrane was deeply damaged and the PWF reduced to 62.0 L/m2.h after healing. Additionally, the tartrazine rejection dropped from 84.7 % to 8.0 % at a higher load of 500 g and recovered to 15.3 % after healing. According to Chen et al. (2020), a larger damage area was formed when the membrane was damaged with higher loads. The membrane required more external forces and the polyelectrolytes in the undamaged area was not enough to heal the damaged site, therefore a lower healing efficiency and recovery were obtained [13].

Fig. 8 compares the salt rejection of the fabricated membranes prepared with and without NCQDs using salt solutions of monovalent and divalent anions. It was observed that the rejection improved with the incorporation of NCQDs. For instance, the rejection of Na2SO4 increased from 70.4 % (without NCQDs) to 98.6 % (with NCQDs). Followed by the rejection of MgSO4 from 59.5 % to 85.7 % and lastly the rejection of NaCl increases from 35.3 % to 73.1 %. In this study, the rejection of the divalent anion performed better than the monovalent anion. From Fig. 8, the divalent sulphate (SO4 2−) anion has the highest rejection due to the greatest negative co-ion charges. The negatively charged functional groups of PAA and NCQDS as the last deposited layer helped to repel the salt anions.

Suhalim et al. (2022) reviewed that the Donnan exclusion mainly governs the rejection of divalent ions. This mechanism occurs through the electrostatic interaction between ionic solutes and charges of the membrane surface [52]. The salt rejection in this study decreases in the sequence of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > NaCl, which is explained by the Donnan exclusion where the rejection of ions increases with higher co-ion charges and decreases with higher counter-ion charges. Additionally, the study claimed the removal process of a membrane is also supported by the steric hindrance, commonly known as size exclusion. This sieving mechanism prevents the particles from passing through the pores that are smaller than the particle size [52]. The addition of NCQDs into the polyelectrolyte matrix served as a barrier which can be observed through the decreasing PWF analysis and increasing tartrazine rejection. The divalent SO4 2− ion is bigger than the monovalent chloride (Cl−) anion, where the larger SO4 2− ions were significantly repelled more than the smaller Cl− ions.

Fig. 9 shows the pH stability of the membrane based on PWF. The membrane without NCQDs (PES/PEI/PAA) and with NCQDs (PES/0.6NCQDs-PEI/0.4NCQDs-PAA) were tested in different pH conditions of pH 4 (HNO3), pH 7 (distilled water) and pH 10 (NaOH). It was observed that the PWF of the membrane without NCQDs increased drastically when the membrane was immersed in the acidic and alkaline solution for two weeks. At pH 4, the PWF increased by 80.4 % from 18.4 L/m2.h to 93.8 L/m2.h while the PWF rose from 17.0 L/m2.h to 76.3 L/m2.h by 77.8 % at pH 10. This result was mainly due to the disintegration of the polyelectrolyte layer. According to Petrila et al. (2021), PEI and PAA are weak polyelectrolytes which are sensitive to surrounding pH [53]. In the study of Elshof et al. (2020), the PAA became uncharged as it lost its charging properties at a lower pH and the PEI loses its charge at a higher pH. The polymer chain was unstable because of the malfunctioning electrostatic attraction between both polyelectrolytes. The polyelectrolyte layer dissociated and led to high PWF as depicted in Fig. 9 [54].

The membrane with NCQDs exhibited better stability at different pH conditions. From Fig. 9, minor changes in PWF were observed when the membrane was immersed in the pH 4, 7 and 10 solutions. The slight increment in the water flux could be due to the high hydration of PAA and hydrophilic NCQDs, resulting in more water molecules permeating through the membrane. In the study of Khan et al. (2018), the N-CDs showed no significant changes of fluorescent intensity when it were stored in solutions of different pH values for 15 days, suggesting that the CDs possess good stability [55]. In this study, the bonding interactions such as the covalent bond, hydrogen bond and electrostatic interactions between the NCQDs and polyelectrolytes are crucial to strengthen the membrane matrix. Lastly, the fabricated membrane with and without NCQDs showed its stability in a neutral condition with a consistent flux after being immersed in distilled water for 24 h.

The anti-fouling performance of the membrane was evaluated by analyzing the reversible and irreversible fouling properties as presented in Fig. 10 . The membrane with NCQDs has a higher reversible fouling (32.42 %) than the membrane without NCQDs (22.03 %). The high electronegativity charge surface of the NCQDs membrane allows the tartrazine foulants on the membrane surface to be removed easily after the cleaning process [28,49]. In addition, the irreversible fouling of the membrane with NCQDs (10.30 %) is lower than the membrane without NCQDs (15.14 %) which led to the easy removal of tartrazine foulants [28].

Table 3 shows the mechanical properties of the modified PES membrane with and without NCQDs. It can be observed that the mechanical properties of both membranes show minor differences, but a slightly better performance for NCQDs-membrane. The small increment in the mechanical properties could be due to the small amount of NCQDs in the polyelectrolyte solutions. According to the study of Arahman. (2018), nanoparticles show a good strengthening effect on the blend PES membrane due to the compact adhesion force [56]. In this study, the membrane was fabricated with a single bilayer polyelectrolyte and NCQDs formed through the LbL method. The impact of nanoparticles could be not significant.

Table 4 compares the membrane performances with other studies. The table shows that the incorporation of nanoparticles has improved the membrane performances such as PWF, solute rejection and anti-fouling properties. Furthermore, the addition of NCQDs into the PEI and PAA solutions in this study significantly increased the self-healing effectiveness of the dual-responsive membrane. The study of Heng et al. (2021) shows NCQDs has anti-fouling and self-cleaning abilities but no polymer for self-healing [19]. The water-responsive self-healing PES/PVA-PAA membrane without nanoparticles achieved a self-healing efficiency of 98.3 % at 48 h of healing time in distilled water which is time-consuming [14]. While another water-responsive self-healing PES/PEI/PAA membrane without nanoparticles only achieved 75.9 % of self-healing efficiency at 6 h of healing time in distilled water [25]. In contrast, the NCQDs membrane with the dual-responsive mechanism in this study improved the self-healing efficiency of 91.1 % (6 h) and 92.7 % (24 h). In addition, the tartrazine rejection also enhanced from 71.9 % to 87.9 % by comparing the PEI/PAA membrane of the previous and current studies. The improvement in membrane performances is mainly due to the presence of various functional groups such as hydroxyl groups, amine groups and carboxyl groups in the NCQDs [32].

In this study, a novel dual-responsive self-healing PES membrane was fabricated by surface modification through the deposition of PEI and PAA, together with NCQDs nanoparticles via electrostatic interactions. The study incorporated 0.6 vol% NCQDs in the PEI and 0.4 vol% in the PAA and, the newly fabricated membrane successfully improved the membrane performance of the previous water-responsive self-healing PES/PEI/PAA membrane. The dual-responsive self-healing mechanism allowed hydrogen bond reformation when in contact with water and the rearrangement of the polymer network when heat is applied. For instance, the self-healing effectiveness rose by 18.1 % from 75.9 % of the old membrane to 91.1 % of the new membrane at 6 h of healing time while the tartrazine recovery increased by 45.8 % from 35.8 % to 63.3 %. Furthermore, the PWF escalated by 47.6 % to 33.8 L/m2.h and the rejection improved by 18.2 % to 87.9 % of tartrazine are repelled. Nonetheless, the membrane's salt rejection, pH stability and anti-fouling properties were enhanced with the involvement of NCQDs in the membrane matrix.
Eng Cheong Wong: Writing – original draft. Woon Chan Chong: Resources. Yean Ling Pang: Writing – review & editing. Ying Hui Ong: Writing – review & editing.
